topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon air hung heavy and still, the kind of quiet that magnifies every small sound. I was taking out the recycling, a mundane chore, when I saw it. Tucked right beside the crumpled junk mail was a card. It was thick, cream-colored, and utterly blank save for a single, hastily scrawled word at the bottom: "Yours." My hand froze mid-lift. A coldness, sharp and immediate, shot up my spine, stealing the breath from my lungs. I dropped the recycling bin with a clatter that seemed deafening in the silence. My heart began hammering against my ribs, a frantic drum solo. I stared at the card, my vision tunneling slightly. Who would send this? And why was it unsigned? Every instinct screamed at me to run, to slam the door and lock everything down, but my feet felt rooted to the linoleum floor. I couldn't move.

[story 2]

It was the sheer audacity of the object that unsettled me. Lying in the slot of the mailbox, catching the weak sunlight, was a card. Unaddressed. Just a single, looping signature written in elegant, unfamiliar script. When I finally retrieved it, my fingers were slick with a sudden clamminess. I held it up to the light, scrutinizing the ink. It looked too deliberate, too personal. A wave of nausea rolled through my stomach, making me want to gag. I backed away slowly, my gaze darting toward the front door, half-expecting someone to materialize in the hallway. My voice, when I finally managed to whisper, was thin and reedy. "No. No, this isn't right." The silence that followed felt vast, expectant, as if the card itself was holding its breath, waiting for me to make the next move.

[story 3]

I found the card wedged between a forgotten utility bill and a flyer for a local bake sale. It was white cardstock, pristine, and bearing a single, looping inscription that felt strangely intimate. As I picked it up, the texture felt wrong—too smooth, almost slick. A tremor started in my hands, running up my forearms until my knuckles were white. My breath hitched; it felt shallow, insufficient. I squeezed my eyes shut for a brief second, trying to force the image away, but the looping script seemed to swim behind my eyelids. I opened them again, and the card seemed to pulse under the light. A profound sense of wrongness settled over me, a deep, internal alarm bell ringing incessantly. I dropped the card instantly, scrambling backward until my shoulders hit the cool plaster of the wall, my gaze fixed on the empty space where the paper had been.
